title: Stealth SYN Scan (Top 1000 Ports)
description: Performs a fast TCP SYN scan against the top 1000 most common ports. This is often referred to as a 'stealth' scan because it never completes the full TCP three-way handshake.
when_to_use: Use this as your initial port scanning technique when you have root/Administrator privileges on your attacking machine and want a rapid overview of open ports without creating full TCP connection logs on the target.
common_pitfalls: Requires root/sudo privileges to construct raw packets. Modern firewalls/IDS will still easily detect this.

title: Full TCP Port Scan with Service Detection
description: Scans all 65,535 TCP ports on a target. It also runs default enumeration scripts (-sC) and attempts to determine the exact version of the services running (-sV) on open ports.
when_to_use: Use this after an initial fast scan reveals some open ports, and you need a comprehensive view of the entire TCP attack surface including obscure high-numbered ports.
common_pitfalls: This scan is extremely slow compared to top-1000 port scans. It is also highly visible and noisy.

title: Top 100 UDP Port Scan
description: Performs a UDP scan against the 100 most commonly used UDP ports. UDP scanning is fundamentally different and often much slower than TCP scanning due to the lack of connection state.
when_to_use: Essential for discovering services that exclusively use UDP, such as SNMP, TFTP, or early versions of DNS, which would be entirely missed by standard TCP scans.
common_pitfalls: Extremely slow. Often blocked by ICMP rate limiting on the target, resulting in many false positives ('open|filtered'). Always verify UDP results.

title: Ping Sweep for Host Discovery
description: Sends ICMP echo requests and TCP ACKs to a subnet to determine which hosts are currently online, without performing any actual port scanning.
when_to_use: Use this when you first land on an internal network segment and need a quick map of live IP addresses to target for deeper enumeration.
common_pitfalls: Windows Firewall blocks ICMP echo requests by default, meaning Windows hosts might appear 'down' even if they are active.

title: Nmap Vulnerability Script Scan
description: Executes a broad suite of Nmap Scripting Engine (NSE) scripts categorized as 'vuln'. These scripts actively check target services for known vulnerabilities, such as MS17-010 or Heartbleed.
when_to_use: Run this after you have identified specific open ports and services, to automatically check if any of them suffer from easily identifiable, high-profile CVEs.
common_pitfalls: These scripts can be very noisy and occasionally crash fragile, legacy services. Not recommended for stealthy red team engagements.

title: High-Speed TCP Port Scan
description: An incredibly fast, asynchronous port scanner capable of scanning the entire internet in under six minutes. It works by bypassing the host operating system's TCP/IP stack and generating raw packets.
when_to_use: Use Masscan when you need to discover open ports across massive external network ranges (e.g., a /16 or /8 subnet) where Nmap would be impractically slow.
common_pitfalls: Setting the rate too high will overwhelm your own router or the target network, causing packet loss and leading you to miss open ports.

title: Query Domain Registration Details
description: Queries the public WHOIS databases to retrieve registration records for a specific domain name, including registrar details, creation/expiration dates, and name servers.
when_to_use: This is one of the very first steps in external OSINT. It helps you identify the target organization's registered infrastructure, hosting providers, and potentially technical contact emails.
common_pitfalls: Modern privacy protections (like GDPR or domain privacy services) often redact the most useful personal contact information from these records.

title: Query All DNS Records
description: Requests all available DNS record types (A, AAAA, MX, TXT, NS, etc.) for a specific domain name from a designated nameserver.
when_to_use: Use this during external reconnaissance to map out subdomains, mail servers, and text records (which sometimes contain sensitive keys or internal IP addresses).
common_pitfalls: Many modern DNS servers block 'ANY' queries to prevent DNS amplification DDoS attacks. You may need to query specific record types (like MX or TXT) individually.

title: DNS Zone Transfer Attempt
description: Attempts a full DNS Zone Transfer (AXFR) from a target nameserver. If misconfigured, the server will dump its entire database of DNS records for the domain to your machine.
when_to_use: Always attempt this when you discover an authoritative nameserver for a target domain. While rare today, a successful zone transfer instantly reveals the organization's entire external (and sometimes internal) DNS footprint.
common_pitfalls: Almost always fails (Refused) on modern networks because zone transfers should only be allowed between authorized primary and secondary DNS servers.

title: Interactive DNS Queries
description: Starts nslookup in an interactive shell, allowing you to continually query different DNS record types (like MX or TXT) against various domains without retyping the command structure.
when_to_use: Particularly useful on restricted Windows environments during post-exploitation where 'dig' is not installed, but you still need to enumerate internal DNS records.
